Patch Set 2: I would prefer that you didn't submit this

Good idea and I don't see anything wrong with the code.

I wonder why not make ldlm_poold to actively work on destroying the locks, though? As in a pretty tight loop with just enough time to let other tasks run (so small sleeps in between)?

Patch Set 5:

Because this question was raised somewhere  Iguess I might ask here to preserve the answer: 
It's clear what happens to such a delayed lock if its' lateer becomes conflicting with something, tht would cancel it right away.

But what about a lock that's blocking something before the export is disconnected? We cannot find such locks unless we iterate per-export list (or obd waiting list comparing it to export, and I ddon't think I saw any of that?) on disconnect, and we do not wait for a long time until it's this locks turn to be removed?

Patch Set 5: I would prefer that you didn't submit this

(10 inline comments)

I was originally against this, since it would just increase the number of conflicting locks kept around after the client unmount, and delay active processes from doing useful work.  In exchange, we speed up unmounting a client, which isn't doing any useful work.

However, I changed my mind and agree this is a useful improvement - essentially the DISCONNECT is an implicit cancellation of all the locks on both the client and server.

I'm not in favor of only cancelling 100 locks per LDLM poold wakeup.  I think it makes sense to aggressively cancel all of the locks as quickly as possible, to reduce the chance of contention, and to minimize resource usage on the server.

Patch Set 14:

Is there a danger of dirty data still existing on the client when it unmounts?  Canceling the locks before unmount ensured that all dirty data is flushed to the server before unmount could finish.

Patch Set 26:

I have to comment that their is a regression in master which without this patch would make it impossible to run IO intensive jobs.

Patch Set 26:

James, could you please explain further, and reference the ticket with the failure and/or what part of this patch fixes it?  As described, this patch is optimizing the time to unmount clients with large numbers of locks, which shouldn't be affecting normal IO operations at all. If there are also bug fixes in this patch, then they aren't described in the commit comment.

Patch Set 26:

If I use "vanilla" master then the problems of LU-6748 make use of the file system impossible. When I add this patch to my master build then the problems of LU-6748 have less of a impact. So this patch is a very good thing.

Patch Set 26:

That seems like papering over an unrelated problem, AFAICS. I'm not against landing this patch for its own merits, but it seems like LU-6748 is a pretty serious problem that needs to be addressed independently of this patch.

Your earlier comment was a bit confusing since I thought this patch was actually fixing some other bug, but it now seems that it is just making the reconnect of a broken client happen more quickly.

Patch Set 26:

I agree this is not the fix for the unrelated problem LU-6478. It just happens that LU-6478 ends up testing the functionality of this patch.

Patch Set 26:

Please review this patch

Patch Set 26:

ping

Patch Set 26:

Hi Andreas,

Could you please review this patch again? Thank you.

Patch Set 26: Code-Review+1

(2 comments)

Test needs some fixing. 

(improvement) almost all of the direct users of ldlm_namespace_free() have been removed by this patch (except obdecho/echo.c?). Better to just get rid of this function entirely and require callers to use *_prior() and *_post and think about how to use them correctly.

That could be done in a follow on patch.

Patch Set 26:

This patch would have been a lot easier to inspect if it was split into several separate parts.  It does has several separate changes that could be split into a patch series.
